The OLD pre-fix async placeholder (`function prompt() { & <scriptPath> }`) had no variable reads at all and was immune to this."

## How to verify

Test directly and precisely — StrictMode's exact behavior around unset `$global:` (as opposed to unset local/script-scope) variables is the crux and must not be assumed either way:

```powershell
pwsh -NoProfile -Command '
Set-StrictMode -Version Latest
if (-not $global:_someNeverSetVar_xyz) { Write-Host "condition evaluated fine, value is falsy as expected" } else { Write-Host "unexpected: truthy" }
Write-Host "no error was thrown"
'
```
If that does NOT throw, also test the EXACT literal line from init.go (copy it verbatim from the file) under `Set-StrictMode -Version Latest`, e.g.:
```powershell
pwsh -NoProfile -Command '
Set-StrictMode -Version Latest
<exact literal trampoline install line from init.go, with a placeholder scriptPath like "C:\test.ps1">
Write-Host "install line ran without throwing"
'
```
Also specifically test `Set-StrictMode -Version 1` and `-Version 2` and `-Version Latest` separately if behavior might differ by version (Microsoft's docs distinguish what each version checks).

Also check: does `Set-StrictMode` set in the CALLING scope (the user's profile / interactive session) even apply inside `Invoke-Expression`'d code, and does it carry into a `function prompt() { ... }` DEFINITION (as opposed to its later invocation) — StrictMode variable-checks happen at execution time of the read, so check whether merely DEFINING the function (which happens immediately) vs. later CALLING it is where the unset-variable read actually occurs, since that changes whether init itself fails vs. only the first prompt draw failing later.

## Report

State CONFIRMED (reproduced an actual throw from StrictMode on this exact code — quote the exact error and which StrictMode version/line triggered it), PLAUSIBLE (some uncertainty about which exact PowerShell version/StrictMode level triggers it, but the mechanism is real for at least one config), or REFUTED (tested thoroughly, StrictMode does NOT throw on unset `$global:` scoped variable reads — quote your test output proving this, and explain why, e.g. StrictMode's uninitialized-variable check may only apply to local/script scope, not explicitly-scoped global reads). Show your actual command output, not just a conclusion.

input.description: Verify: Remove-Module can resurrect the module
input.prompt:
Verify a candidate finding from a code review, empirically, using live PowerShell (`pwsh` is available).

Repo: D:\oh-my-posh. Build the test binary: `cd D:\oh-my-posh\src && go build -o omp-verify2.exe .` (delete when done). Use a minimal async theme config:
```yaml
version: 4
async: true
blocks:
  - type: prompt
    alignment: left
    segments:
      - type: text
        style: plain
        template: "TESTPROMPT"
```

Read src/shell/init.go's `sourceCommandAsync` PWSH case and src/shell/scripts/omp.ps1's OnRemove block (search for `OnRemove +=`, roughly lines 1300-1390) for exact current text.

## Candidate finding to verify

"When `$global:_ompOriginalPromptFunction` was captured as falsy/`$null` (e.g. because the host had no `prompt` function defined at all when the async init line first ran — a genuinely rare edge case, but also reachable if a user does `Remove-Item Function:prompt` before running the init line), then `Remove-Module -Name oh-my-posh-core -Force` can silently RESURRECT the module instead of cleanly removing it. Mechanism claimed: OnRemove's async branch does `$global:_ompPromptFunction = $global:_ompOriginalPromptFunction` (setting it to the falsy/null captured value); the trampoline (which is NEVER removed by Remove-Module — it's a plain global function installed once at init, outside the module) remains installed as `prompt`; on the VERY NEXT prompt draw after removal, the trampoline's `if (-not $global:_ompPromptFunction)` condition is now true again (since it was reset to null/falsy), so it re-triggers the FULL deferred import — reinstalling the module the user just explicitly removed, with no error or indication this happened."

## How to verify

Simulate in `pwsh -NoProfile`:
1. BEFORE running the omp init line at all, ensure there is no `prompt` function: `Remove-Item Function:\prompt -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue` (or check `Test-Path Function:\prompt` first — on a truly fresh `pwsh -NoProfile` session, is there a built-in default `prompt` function already defined, or is it genuinely absent until PowerShell's own default gets JIT-defined on first use? This matters — check `Get-Command prompt -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue` and `$function:prompt` right at session start before doing anything.)
2. Run `$init = & .\omp-verify2.exe init pwsh --config <cfg>; Invoke-Expression ($init -join "`n")`.
3. Check `$global:_ompOriginalPromptFunction` — is it null/empty, given step 1?
4. Call `prompt` (first draw, triggers import). Call `prompt` again (steady state, confirm `TESTPROMPT` renders).
5. Run `Remove-Module -Name oh-my-posh-core -Force`.
6. Check `$global:_ompPromptFunction` right after removal — is it null/falsy?
7. Call `prompt` ONE more time. Does it render `TESTPROMPT` again (meaning the module got silently reinstalled/resurrected), or does it correctly show nothing/a plain prompt (meaning removal stuck)?

Report your actual observations at each numbered step, especially whether PowerShell has a genuinely-undefined `prompt` function at fresh session start (this determines whether the "falsy original" scenario is even reachable in a normal session, or only via the explicit `Remove-Item Function:\prompt` workaround).

## Report

State CONFIRMED (reproduced: after Remove-Module, the next prompt call resurrects the module — show your test output), PLAUSIBLE (mechanism traced correctly in the code but you couldn't get `_ompOriginalPromptFunction` to actually be falsy in a realistic scenario, or some other gap — explain precisely), or REFUTED (tested and Remove-Module cleanly sticks — show output, explain what actually happens, e.g. maybe PowerShell always has SOME default prompt function so this is unreachable in practice). Clean up omp-verify2.exe when done.

## What I tested

The literal trampoline from `D:\oh-my-posh\src\shell\init.go` (`sourceCommandAsync`, PWSH case):

```
function prompt() { if (-not $global:_ompPromptFunction) { $global:_ompAsyncInit = $true; & %s; return }; if ($global:_ompPromptFunction) { & $global:_ompPromptFunction } }
```

and the real delegation shape from `D:\oh-my-posh\src\shell\scripts\omp.ps1` line 1006 (`$global:_ompPromptFunction = $promptFunction`, a module-bound scriptblock whose first real statement stores `$?` — line ~915).

Ran in `pwsh -NoProfile` on this machine, with the delegated function reading `$?` as its first statement after a failed `Get-Item 'Z:\does-not-exist-xyz' -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue`, in four shapes: plain scriptblock, module-bound scriptblock (via `New-Module`, matching omp.ps1 exactly), engine-style `$ExecutionContext.SessionState.InvokeCommand.InvokeScript('prompt')`, and a native-command failure (`cmd /c "exit 5"`).

## Actual output

```
=== Test A: module-bound scriptblock through exact trampoline ===
  [module fn] OriginalLastExecutionStatus = False
=== Test B: sanity, after a SUCCESSFUL command ===
  [module fn] OriginalLastExecutionStatus = True
=== Test C: engine-style invocation (InvokeCommand on 'prompt'), after failure ===
  [module fn] OriginalLastExecutionStatus = False
=== Test D: native command failure (exit code), after failure ===
  [module fn] OriginalLastExecutionStatus = False (LASTEXITCODE=5)
```

`$?` correctly propagates as `$false` through both trampoline `if`s into the delegated real prompt function, in every invocation shape. Success correctly reads `$true` (Test B), so the test isn't stuck-at-false.

## Why the claim is wrong (the interesting part)

The mechanism is half-real, which is presumably what made it convincing. I isolated it:

```
=== Test 4: does evaluating an if-condition variable test reset $? ===
  inside condtest: $? on entry = False, $? after if-condition eval = True
```

The `if (-not $global:_ompPromptFunction)` condition DOES reset `$?` to `$true` — but only as observed by subsequent statements **in the trampoline's own scope**. When `& $global:_ompPromptFunction` invokes the delegated scriptblock, PowerShell gives the callee's first statement the `$?` of the last completed *pipeline* (the user's failed command), not the trampoline's intervening expression evaluations. So the value the omp `$promptFunction` stores in `$script:OriginalLastExecutionStatus` matches the user's real command status.

Confidence in REFUTED is high: exact trampoline text, exact module-bound delegation, engine-style invocation, both failure kinds, plus a positive control — all consistent.</result>

The finding is real and reproduced empirically at every StrictMode level, including 1.0.

### Exact code under test (verbatim from `D:\oh-my-posh\src\shell\init.go`, `sourceCommandAsync`, PWSH case, lines 285-289)

```

```

### Test 1 — unset `$global:` read under each StrictMode version (pwsh -NoProfile)

```
--- StrictMode 1.0 ---
THREW on global: read -> The variable '$global:_someNeverSetVar_xyz' cannot be retrieved because it has not been set.
--- StrictMode 2.0 ---
THREW on global: read -> The variable '$global:_someNeverSetVar_xyz' cannot be retrieved because it has not been set.
--- StrictMode 3.0 ---
THREW ...
--- StrictMode Latest ---
THREW ...
```

The explicit `global:` scope qualifier does NOT exempt the read — StrictMode's uninitialized-variable check applies to explicitly-scoped reads too, and even version 1.0 triggers it (1.0 only exempts uninitialized variables inside strings).

### Test 2 — the exact literal init line, `Set-StrictMode -Version Latest` in caller, then `Invoke-Expression` (the real init path)

```
INSTALL THREW -> The variable '$global:_ompOriginalPromptFunction' cannot be retrieved because it has not been set.
prompt function exists: "PS $($executionContext.SessionState.Path.Curren...   <- still the DEFAULT pwsh prompt
ompOriginalPromptFunction set? False
```

Repeated with `-Version 1.0` and `-Version 2.0`: identical throw both times.

### Key mechanics confirmed

1. **StrictMode set in the calling scope DOES apply inside `Invoke-Expression`'d code** — the IEX'd init line inherits the session's strict mode.
2. **The failure is at init time, not first-prompt time.** The throw happens in the very first statement (`if (-not $global:_ompOriginalPromptFunction)`), which executes immediately. It is a terminating error, so the rest of the IEX'd text — including the `function prompt() { ... }` definition — never executes. Test 2 proves it: `prompt` remained the built-in default and `_ompOriginalPromptFunction` was never set. oh-my-posh never installs its trampoline at all.
3. The function *body*'s own reads of `$global:_ompPromptFunction` would be a second, later failure point (at first prompt draw), but it's moot — init aborts before the function is defined.
4. The old placeholder `function prompt() { & <scriptPath> }` contains no variable reads, so it was immune, matching the finding's claim.

Trivial repro one-liner: `pwsh -NoProfile -Command "Set-StrictMode -Version 1; if (-not \$global:_x) { 'ok' }"` → throws.

Fix direction (not applied): use a read that StrictMode permits, e.g. `if (-not (Test-Path Variable:global:_ompOriginalPromptFunction))` or `Get-Variable -Name _ompOriginalPromptFunction -Scope Global -ErrorAction Ignore`, for every first-read of the three globals (`_ompOriginalPromptFunction`, `_ompPromptFunction`, and `_ompAsyncInit`'s later consumers in the generated script would need the same audit).

The resurrection bug is real and reproduced end-to-end in live `pwsh -NoProfile`, but only reachable when `prompt` was explicitly removed before init — a fresh session always has a default `prompt` function, so the normal path is safe.

### Observations per step

**Step 1 — fresh session state.** A fresh `pwsh -NoProfile` session DOES have `prompt` already defined as a real function at startup (`Test-Path Function:\prompt -> True`, `Get-Command prompt -> Function` with the standard `"PS $($executionContext...)>"` body). It is not JIT-defined on first draw. So `$Function:prompt` is a non-null, truthy ScriptBlock in any normal session, and the falsy-original scenario is **only reachable via `Remove-Item Function:\prompt` before the init line** (which the test then did: `Test-Path -> False`).

**Steps 2-3.** After the init line, `$global:_ompOriginalPromptFunction` was `null? True; falsy? True` — the `if (-not $global:_ompOriginalPromptFunction) { ... = $Function:prompt }` guard in init.go:285 captured `$null`.

**Step 4.** First `prompt` call triggered the deferred import (module loaded, empty first draw), second call rendered `TESTPROMPT`. Normal async behavior.

**Steps 5-6.** `Remove-Module -Name oh-my-posh-core -Force` succeeded (module gone). But OnRemove's async branch (omp.ps1:1357, `$global:_ompPromptFunction = $global:_ompOriginalPromptFunction`) set `_ompPromptFunction` back to `$null` — `null? True; falsy? True`.

**Step 7 — resurrection.** The very next `prompt` call hit the trampoline's `if (-not $global:_ompPromptFunction)` branch and silently re-ran the full init: `module resurrected? True`, and the call after that rendered `TESTPROMPT` again. No error, no indication.

### Control run (default prompt intact)

Same flow without removing `prompt` first: original capture truthy; after `Remove-Module` the post-removal draw was the restored default `PS D:\oh-my-posh> `, `module resurrected? False`, and it stayed unloaded. Removal sticks cleanly in the normal case.

### Verdict detail

The claimed mechanism is exactly right: the trampoline installed by `sourceCommandAsync` (D:\oh-my-posh\src\shell\init.go:283-289) is a plain global function that survives `Remove-Module`, and it treats a falsy `$global:_ompPromptFunction` as "not yet initialized" — indistinguishable from "user restored a null original". Severity caveat: since PowerShell always defines a default `prompt` at session start, the trigger requires a user who deleted `prompt` (or a profile that does) before running the init line, so it's an edge case, not a mainline regression. A fix would need OnRemove to distinguish "restore null original" — e.g. restore `$Function:prompt` to the original (or remove the trampoline entirely) instead of relying on the sentinel variable.

The finding reproduces exactly as described, on a real build of HEAD in a clean `pwsh -NoProfile` session. After a sync-to-async transition, omp stops rendering entirely: the prompt reverts to the pre-omp prompt and stays there.

### Test output (verbatim)

Harness: pre-omp prompt is `function global:prompt { "PREOMP> " }`; sync config renders `TESTPROMPT`; async config is identical plus `async: true`; isolated cache via `OMP_CACHE_DIR`.

```
STEP 1 prompt output: <<TESTPROMPT>>                 (sync init works)
STEP 2 _ompOriginalPromptFunction captured:
  "# store the original last command execution status / if ($global:NVS_ORIGINAL_LASTEXECUTIONSTATUS ..."
                                                     (that's omp's OWN sync prompt body, not PREOMP)
STEP 3 first draw output: <<>>                       (trampoline fires deferred import)
STEP 3 function:prompt definition after first draw:
  "PREOMP> "                                         (trampoline is GONE, replaced by pre-omp prompt)
STEP 4 second draw output: <<PREOMP> >>
STEP 4 contains TESTPROMPT: False
STEP 4 _ompPromptFunction set: True
STEP 4 direct _ompPromptFunction call output: <<TESTPROMPT>>
STEP 4 is function:prompt the trampoline: False
```

### Every sub-claim verified

- (a) Stale original capture: after the async re-source, `$global:_ompOriginalPromptFunction` holds omp's own sync prompt function (its body contains omp internals like `NVS_ORIGINAL_LASTEXECUTIONSTATUS`), not the true pre-omp prompt. Confirmed at STEP 2.

- (c) On first draw the trampoline runs the deferred init script; its preamble (`D:\oh-my-posh\src\shell\scripts\omp.ps1` lines 1-4) does `Remove-Module -Name "oh-my-posh-core" -Force`. - The reverse transition (async → sync) is not affected by this exact mechanism: the async-mode OnRemove branch only touches `$global:_ompPromptFunction`, and the sync init path unconditionally sets `$Function:prompt` (line 1009).

findings.summary: The async trampoline's first statement reads an unset $global: variable, which throws under Set-StrictMode, aborting init before the trampoline is ever installed
findings.short_summary: StrictMode profile breaks async pwsh init entirely
findings.failure_scenario: A user with `Set-StrictMode -Version 1` (or 2/Latest) earlier in their PowerShell profile runs `oh-my-posh init pwsh --config X | iex` with `async: true`. The emitted line's `if (-not $global:_ompOriginalPromptFunction) { ... }` reads a global that has never been assigned; StrictMode throws "The variable '$global:_ompOriginalPromptFunction' cannot be retrieved because it has not been set" as a terminating error, so the rest of the line — including the `function prompt() {...}` definition — never executes. Verified live: reproduced at StrictMode versions 1.0, 2.0, 3.0, and Latest; the old pre-fix placeholder had no variable reads and was immune.

findings.summary: Switching a live session from sync to async mode re-clobbers the trampoline via the OLD sync module's OnRemove branch, permanently silencing omp
findings.short_summary: Sync-to-async mid-session switch kills the prompt
findings.failure_scenario: User has a sync session running, flips `async: true` in config, re-sources the init line. The trampoline installs and captures `$global:_ompOriginalPromptFunction = $Function:prompt` — but that's omp's OWN sync prompt function, not the true original. On the first draw, the deferred script's `Remove-Module -Force` preamble fires the OLD module's OnRemove; that instance's `$script:AsyncInit` is `$false` (it loaded sync), so it takes the sync branch and does `$Function:prompt = $script:OriginalPromptFunction`, overwriting the just-installed trampoline with the old module's own pre-omp backup. The new async module then only ever writes `$global:_ompPromptFunction`, which nothing calls anymore. Verified live end-to-end: second draw after the switch renders the pre-omp prompt, not the theme, and stays that way.

findings.summary: OnRemove's async restore reuses the same falsy sentinel the trampoline treats as "not yet initialized", so Remove-Module can silently resurrect the module it just removed
findings.failure_scenario: If `$global:_ompOriginalPromptFunction` was captured as falsy (reachable when `Remove-Item Function:\prompt` ran before the init line — not reachable in a normal session, since pwsh always defines a default `prompt`), `Remove-Module -Name oh-my-posh-core -Force` sets `$global:_ompPromptFunction` back to that falsy value via OnRemove. The trampoline (never removed by Remove-Module — it's a plain global function outside the module) treats that falsy value as "not yet initialized" on the next draw and silently re-runs the full deferred import, reinstalling the just-removed module with no error. Verified live: reproduced end-to-end; the normal case (default prompt present) removes cleanly, confirmed by a control run.

findings.summary: The trampoline's second `if ($global:_ompPromptFunction)` guard is unreachable-as-false within the same invocation and can never fire differently from the first check
findings.short_summary: Second trampoline guard is provably dead code
findings.failure_scenario: The first block always ends in `return`, so the second `if` is only ever reached when the first condition was false — meaning `$global:_ompPromptFunction` is already known truthy, unchanged in between (single-threaded, no intervening statement touches it). A reader will assume it guards a real third state (import ran but didn't set the pointer) that cannot occur in this control flow; `if (-not $X) { ...; return }; & $X` is behaviorally identical with one fewer conditional.

findings.summary: $global:_ompAsyncInit is the only $global:_omp* flag not declared in the existing global-enablers block, with no comment there warning why it can't be
findings.failure_scenario: Every other $global:_omp* flag (_ompJobCount, _ompFTCSMarks, _ompStreaming, etc.) is declared with a $false default at lines 20-27 and flipped by feature snippets. _ompAsyncInit is instead created ad hoc from the Go-emitted trampoline string with no matching declaration here — for a real reason (that block runs after the trampoline sets the flag, so a $false default there would stomp it before line 44 consumes it), but that reason is undocumented at the block itself. A future contributor "tidying" the globals for consistency adds `$global:_ompAsyncInit = $false` to the enablers block, silently forcing the sync branch in async mode and reintroducing the exact prompt-clobbering bug #7714 fixed.

findings.summary: The new test asserts the emitted trampoline string equals a copy of itself, so it cannot catch any of the confirmed behavioral bugs above or future drift between init.go and omp.ps1's variable names
findings.short_summary: New test checks string equality, not behavior
findings.failure_scenario: TestSourceCommandAsyncPwsh pins the exact Go output but never executes it, so it stays green through all three confirmed bugs (StrictMode throw, sync-to-async clobber, Remove-Module resurrection) and would stay green if a future edit renamed `_ompPromptFunction` in omp.ps1 without updating init.go's string, silently reverting every draw to a full re-import.

if ($script:AsyncInit) {
        # Never touch the global prompt binding after the trampoline installed
        # it - anything wrapping it (e.g. another tool's prompt hook) must
        # keep a valid reference forever. Export-ModuleMember below becomes a
        # silent no-op with no function named "prompt" in this module, which
        # is intentional. $global:_ompInitialized is deliberately separate
        # from $global:_ompPromptFunction: it only ever means "don't
        # re-import", so OnRemove can restore a falsy original prompt without
        # the trampoline mistaking that for "never initialized" and silently
        # reinstalling this module on the next draw.
        $global:_ompPromptFunction = $promptFunction
        $global:_ompInitialized = $true
    }
    else {
        $Function:prompt = $promptFunction
    }
